title: ARTERIOVENOUS (AV) FISTULA CARE and CHRONIC HEMODIALYSIS SELF-MANAGEMENT in a 58-Year-Old Man with ESRD from Diabetic Nephropathy ~3 Months Into Maintenance Hemodialysis via a Tunneled CATHETER Who Has a NEW LEFT-FOREARM AV FISTULA (Created 8 Weeks Ago, Now Maturing) Being Evaluated for First Cannulation: WHY a FISTULA Is Preferred Over a Graft Over a CATHETER ('Fistula First' — Lowest Infection/Clot Risk), Assessing PATENCY (a Palpable THRILL + an Audible BRUIT) and MATURATION, the High-Yield ACCESS-ARM PRECAUTIONS (NO Blood Pressure, NO Venipuncture/IV/Blood Draws, NO Tight Clothing/Jewelry, Don't Sleep On It or Carry Heavy Loads — and Daily Self-Check of the Thrill), Recognizing COMPLICATIONS (LOSS of Thrill/Bruit = THROMBOSIS Emergency, Infection, STEAL Syndrome with a Cold/Painful Hand, Aneurysm/Pseudoaneurysm, Prolonged Post-Dialysis Bleeding), and INTERDIALYTIC SELF-CARE (Fluid + Sodium + POTASSIUM + PHOSPHORUS Restriction, PHOSPHATE BINDERS WITH Meals, an Interdialytic Weight-Gain Target, Holding Antihypertensives/Giving Dialyzable Meds AFTER Dialysis, Anemia/ESA + Iron, and NEVER Skipping Sessions) — Plus the CATHETER Infection Risk That Makes Transitioning to the Fistula a Priority
subtitle: For a patient with end-stage renal disease (ESRD) on maintenance HEMODIALYSIS, the vascular ACCESS is a lifeline, and the order of preference is 'FISTULA FIRST': an arteriovenous (AV) FISTULA (a surgically created artery-to-vein anastomosis, usually in the non-dominant forearm) has the LOWEST risk of infection and clotting and the best long-term patency, followed by an AV GRAFT (a synthetic conduit, usable sooner but higher clot/infection risk), with a tunneled central CATHETER LAST because it carries the HIGHEST infection (CLABSI/sepsis) and thrombosis risk. This case follows C. Ramos, a 58-year-old man with ESRD from diabetic nephropathy who began maintenance hemodialysis about 3 months ago through a tunneled catheter and had a LEFT-FOREARM AV FISTULA created 8 weeks ago; it is now MATURING (a fistula typically needs ~6–12 weeks before first cannulation while the vein 'arterializes' — enlarges and toughens) and is being evaluated for first use so the higher-risk catheter can be removed. The nurse ASSESSES the access for patency at every encounter — a palpable THRILL (a continuous buzzing/vibration) and an audible BRUIT (a continuous swooshing sound on auscultation) confirm flow — and checks for maturation (a well-developed, easily palpable vein). The nurse teaches the high-yield ACCESS-ARM PRECAUTIONS that protect the fistula: NO blood pressure measurements, NO venipuncture/IV starts/blood draws, and NO injections in that arm; avoid tight clothing, jewelry, watches, or a name band on that wrist; don't sleep on the arm, carry heavy bags on it, or let it be compressed; and CHECK THE THRILL DAILY (report any loss). The patient is taught to recognize COMPLICATIONS: LOSS of the thrill/bruit signals THROMBOSIS (a surgical emergency — notify immediately to attempt salvage); redness/warmth/drainage/fever = INFECTION; a cold, pale, painful, numb hand = vascular STEAL syndrome; a bulging pulsatile area = aneurysm/pseudoaneurysm; and bleeding that won't stop after dialysis needs firm pressure and escalation. Between treatments, SELF-MANAGEMENT is central: restrict FLUID and SODIUM (to limit interdialytic weight gain, targeting roughly ≤1–1.5 kg/day or per the renal team), restrict POTASSIUM (avoid high-K foods — oranges, bananas, potatoes, tomatoes, salt substitutes) and PHOSPHORUS, take PHOSPHATE BINDERS WITH meals (to bind dietary phosphate in the gut, not between meals), follow the renal diet with adequate protein for dialysis patients, take ERYTHROPOIESIS-STIMULATING AGENTS and iron for anemia and active vitamin D/binders for mineral-bone disease, generally HOLD antihypertensives before a session and give DIALYZABLE medications AFTER dialysis (per protocol), and NEVER skip dialysis sessions (missed sessions cause dangerous fluid overload and HYPERKALEMIA). Step-6 evaluates whether each later finding reflects the patient IMPROVING, WORSENING, or remaining UNCHANGED.

patient.name: C. Ramos
patient.admitDate: ESRD from diabetic nephropathy; began maintenance hemodialysis ~3 months ago via a right internal jugular tunneled catheter. A left-forearm AV fistula was created 8 weeks ago and is now maturing; seen in the dialysis unit today for routine treatment, access assessment, and self-care teaching, with a plan to begin cannulating the fistula and remove the catheter once it is reliably usable. Dialyzes Monday/Wednesday/Friday.
patient.room: Outpatient Hemodialysis Unit, Chair 4; wife present for teaching

vitals.spo2: 98% on room air; T 36.8°C; up 2.6 kg since the last session (interdialytic fluid gain) with mild pedal edema and a few bibasilar crackles; pre-dialysis hypertension; reports he 'had soup and a banana' over the weekend

vitals.bp: 166/90 (taken in the RIGHT arm — never the fistula arm)
vitals.spo2: 98% RA; palpable continuous THRILL and audible continuous BRUIT over the maturing left-forearm fistula; vein developing well; warm pink hand with strong distal pulse and brisk capillary refill (no steal); no redness, drainage, or aneurysmal bulge; catheter exit site clean/dry/intact

99% RA; T 36.7°C; at estimated dry weight, edema reduced, crackles cleared; fistula thrill/bruit intact after the run; held his morning antihypertensive before dialysis as ordered and took dialyzable medications after the session

99% RA; T 36.8°C; smaller interdialytic weight gains (~1.3 kg) with better fluid/sodium control; takes phosphate binders with meals; protecting the access arm correctly; fistula cannulated successfully this week with a plan to remove the catheter soon

vitals.time: ALTERNATE course — access complication (WRONG practice/red flag)
vitals.spo2: 97% RA; T 38.4°C; after a blood pressure cuff was repeatedly placed on the fistula arm, the THRILL and BRUIT are now ABSENT and the area is firm — concern for fistula THROMBOSIS; separately, the catheter exit site is red with purulent drainage and he is febrile — catheter-related bloodstream infection

vitals.time: Recovery — established well-functioning fistula, catheter removed
vitals.spo2: 99% RA; T 36.8°C; mature fistula reliably cannulated for each session, catheter removed (infection risk eliminated), stable dry weight, controlled potassium/phosphorus, adherent to the M/W/F schedule and access-arm precautions

labs.n: PRESENTATION + WHY 'FISTULA FIRST' (access types, ranking, and maturation)
labs.v: 58 y/o man, ESRD from diabetic nephropathy, ~3 months on maintenance HD via a tunneled CATHETER, with a NEW left-forearm AV fistula (8 weeks old) now maturing and being evaluated for first cannulation so the catheter can be removed. KEY PRINCIPLE — 'FISTULA FIRST': vascular-access preference is AV FISTULA (artery-to-vein anastomosis; lowest infection + clot risk, best long-term patency, but needs ~6–12 weeks to MATURE before use as the vein 'arterializes') > AV GRAFT (synthetic conduit; usable sooner ~2–4 weeks but higher clot/infection risk) > CENTRAL CATHETER (tunneled; usable immediately but the HIGHEST infection [CLABSI/sepsis] and thrombosis risk) — so transitioning OFF the catheter onto the fistula is a safety priority. Maturation is promoted by hand-exercise (e.g., squeezing a ball) per the surgeon and protected by avoiding any compression of the arm.
labs.r: FISTULA FIRST: AV fistula (lowest infection/clot risk, best patency; matures ~6–12 wks) > AV graft (usable ~2–4 wks, higher clot/infection risk) > catheter (immediate but HIGHEST infection/thrombosis risk). Get off the catheter onto the fistula ASAP. Maturation needs weeks of arterialization + hand exercises; protect the arm from compression.

labs.v: ASSESS PATENCY every shift/encounter: PALPATE for a continuous THRILL (a buzzing vibration) and AUSCULTATE for a continuous BRUIT (a swooshing sound) — both present = flowing/patent. PROTECT the access arm (teach the patient): NO blood pressure cuff, NO venipuncture/IV starts/blood draws, NO injections in that arm; NO tight clothing, jewelry, watch, or wristband on that side; do NOT sleep on the arm, carry heavy bags, or otherwise compress it; keep it clean. The patient should CHECK THE THRILL DAILY (feel for the buzz) and report any change immediately. Post-dialysis, hold the cannulation sites with gentle firm pressure until hemostasis (don't occlude flow), and keep the dressing dry.
labs.r: Patency = palpable THRILL + audible BRUIT (check every encounter; teach DAILY self-check). ACCESS-ARM PRECAUTIONS: NO BP, NO venipuncture/IV/blood draw/injection in that arm; no tight clothing/jewelry/wristband; don't sleep on it, compress it, or carry heavy loads. Post-HD: firm (not occlusive) pressure to sites until hemostasis.

labs.v: THROMBOSIS/clotting: LOSS of the thrill and bruit (no buzz/swoosh) = the access is clotting — a SURGICAL EMERGENCY; notify immediately for possible thrombectomy/declotting to salvage it. INFECTION: redness, warmth, swelling, drainage, or fever at the site (catheters are especially high risk → CLABSI/sepsis) → cultures + antibiotics. VASCULAR 'STEAL' SYNDROME: the fistula diverts arterial flow from the hand → a COLD, PALE, PAINFUL, NUMB hand with weak distal pulse → report (may need flow-limiting surgery). ANEURYSM/PSEUDOANEURYSM: a bulging, pulsatile area from repeated cannulation → protect and monitor; rapid enlargement/thinning skin/bleeding is urgent. PROLONGED BLEEDING after dialysis (heparin + repeated punctures): apply firm pressure and escalate if it won't stop. HIGH-OUTPUT cardiac strain is a rarer late complication.
labs.r: Red flags: NO thrill/bruit = THROMBOSIS (emergency — notify to salvage); redness/warmth/drainage/fever = INFECTION (catheters worst); cold/pale/painful/numb hand = STEAL syndrome; pulsatile bulge = aneurysm/pseudoaneurysm; won't-stop post-HD bleeding = firm pressure + escalate.

labs.n: INTERDIALYTIC SELF-MANAGEMENT — fluid, diet, binders, meds, schedule
labs.v: FLUID + SODIUM restriction limits interdialytic weight gain (target roughly ≤1–1.5 kg/day or per the team) and pre-dialysis hypertension/edema; weigh daily and track. POTASSIUM restriction (AVOID high-K foods — oranges, bananas, potatoes, tomatoes, melons, dried fruit, and SALT SUBSTITUTES [KCl]) prevents life-threatening hyperkalemia between runs. PHOSPHORUS restriction (limit dairy, nuts, cola, processed foods) plus PHOSPHATE BINDERS taken WITH meals (binders work in the gut on food phosphate — NOT between meals) controls mineral-bone disease; active vitamin D as prescribed. Adequate PROTEIN is needed for dialysis patients (dialysis removes protein — this is NOT the protein-restricted pre-dialysis CKD diet). ANEMIA of CKD: erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (epoetin/darbepoetin) + iron. MEDS: generally HOLD antihypertensives before a session (to avoid intradialytic hypotension) and give DIALYZABLE drugs (many water-soluble agents, some antibiotics) AFTER dialysis — per protocol. NEVER skip sessions: missed dialysis → fluid overload, pulmonary edema, and dangerous HYPERKALEMIA.
labs.r: Restrict FLUID + Na (weight gain ≤~1–1.5 kg/day), K (no oranges/bananas/potatoes/tomatoes/salt substitutes), and PHOSPHORUS; PHOSPHATE BINDERS WITH meals; ADEQUATE protein (dialysis removes it — not the pre-dialysis low-protein diet); ESA + iron for anemia; HOLD antihypertensives pre-HD + give dialyzable meds AFTER; daily weights; NEVER skip sessions (→ overload + hyperkalemia).

labs.n: CATHETER risk, transition to the fistula, and PERTINENT NEGATIVES (what NOT to do)
labs.v: The tunneled CATHETER carries the highest infection and clotting risk, so converting to the maturing fistula and removing the catheter is a priority once the fistula is reliably usable; meanwhile use strict aseptic catheter care, keep the dressing dry/intact, and NEVER use the dialysis catheter for routine blood draws or IV fluids unless specifically authorized. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice — these are WRONG): do NOT take blood pressures, draw blood, start IVs, or give injections in the fistula arm; do NOT apply tight bands, BP cuffs, or restrictive jewelry to that arm; do NOT ignore a lost thrill/bruit (it is an emergency, not 'wait and see'); do NOT keep the catheter longer than necessary 'because it works'; do NOT take phosphate binders between meals (they must be taken WITH food); do NOT put the patient on a protein-restricted diet (that is for pre-dialysis CKD); and do NOT let the patient skip dialysis sessions.
labs.r: Catheter = highest infection/clot risk → transition to fistula + remove catheter ASAP; aseptic catheter care, dressing dry, don't use it for routine draws/IV. NEGATIVES: no BP/venipuncture/IV/injection/tight band on the fistula arm; don't ignore a lost thrill/bruit (emergency); don't keep the catheter unnecessarily; don't take binders between meals; don't protein-restrict a dialysis patient; don't skip sessions.

labs.v: 5.8 mmol/L (elevated — reflects the weekend dietary potassium and interdialytic accumulation; dialysis will correct it, and diet teaching is reinforced; watch for peaked T waves/arrhythmia)
6.2 mg/dL (elevated — inadequate phosphate binding/dietary phosphorus; reinforce binders WITH meals and phosphorus-restricted diet to prevent renal osteodystrophy and vascular calcification)
8.2 mg/dL (low-normal/low — part of CKD mineral-bone disease; managed with active vitamin D and binder selection, balanced against phosphorus)
9.5 g/dL (low — anemia of chronic kidney disease from reduced erythropoietin; treated with an ESA plus iron, with a deliberately conservative target, NOT normalization)
19 mmol/L (low — metabolic acidosis of ESRD between sessions; corrected by dialysis)

nursesNote: Outpatient hemodialysis unit — ESRD (diabetic nephropathy), ~3 months on HD via a right IJ tunneled catheter, with a new left-forearm AV fistula (8 weeks old) maturing and being evaluated for first cannulation. Pre-dialysis BP 168/92 (taken in the RIGHT arm — never the fistula arm), up 2.6 kg since last session with mild pedal edema and bibasilar crackles (interdialytic fluid/sodium excess — had soup and a banana over the weekend). Left-forearm fistula with a palpable continuous THRILL and audible BRUIT, vein developing well; hand warm/pink with strong pulse and brisk refill (no steal); no redness/drainage/aneurysm; catheter exit site clean/dry/intact. Reinforced ACCESS-ARM PRECAUTIONS: no BP, venipuncture, IV, or injections in the left arm; no tight clothing/jewelry/wristband; don't sleep on it or carry heavy loads; CHECK THE THRILL DAILY and report any loss; do hand exercises to promote maturation. Taught complication red flags: loss of thrill/bruit = thrombosis emergency; redness/warmth/drainage/fever = infection; cold/pale/painful/numb hand = steal; pulsatile bulge = aneurysm; won't-stop bleeding after dialysis = firm pressure + escalate. Reviewed interdialytic self-care: fluid/sodium restriction with daily weights (target ≤~1–1.5 kg/day gain), potassium restriction (avoid oranges/bananas/potatoes/tomatoes/salt substitutes — pre-HD K 5.8), phosphorus restriction with PHOSPHATE BINDERS taken WITH meals (phosphorus 6.2), adequate protein (dialysis patient — NOT the pre-dialysis low-protein diet), ESA + iron for anemia (Hgb 9.5), hold the morning antihypertensive before the session and take dialyzable meds after, and NEVER skip sessions. Post-dialysis at target dry weight, BP 138/82, edema/crackles improved, fistula thrill/bruit intact. Plan to cannulate the fistula and remove the catheter once reliably usable. Wife included in teaching with teach-back.
providerOrders: ACCESS ASSESSMENT: assess the AV fistula every encounter for a palpable thrill and audible bruit, maturation, and signs of infection/steal/aneurysm; document patency
ACCESS-ARM PROTECTION: NO blood pressure, venipuncture, IV starts, or injections in the left (fistula) arm; place a limb-alert band on the OPPOSITE arm; avoid constriction of the access arm

FISTULA-FIRST TRANSITION: evaluate the maturing fistula for cannulation and remove the tunneled catheter once the fistula is reliably usable; until then, strict aseptic catheter care, keep the dressing dry/intact, and do not use the dialysis catheter for routine draws/IV unless authorized
DIALYSIS PRESCRIPTION: hemodialysis per schedule (M/W/F) to estimated dry weight; monitor for intradialytic hypotension/cramping; pre- and post-dialysis weights and vital signs

MEDICATIONS: hold antihypertensives before dialysis per protocol; administer dialyzable medications after dialysis; ESA (epoetin/darbepoetin) + iron for anemia of CKD; active vitamin D and phosphate binders WITH meals
DIET/FLUID: renal diet — fluid and sodium restriction (limit interdialytic weight gain), potassium restriction, phosphorus restriction, and adequate protein for a dialysis patient; daily weights
LABS: monitor potassium, phosphorus, calcium, bicarbonate, hemoglobin/iron studies, and Kt/V adequacy per protocol

COMPLICATION ESCALATION: report immediately any loss of thrill/bruit (possible thrombosis — emergency), access infection signs, a cold/painful/numb hand (steal), an enlarging pulsatile bulge, or bleeding that does not stop with firm pressure

EDUCATION: teach access-arm precautions, daily thrill self-check, complication red flags, phosphate-binder timing with meals, fluid/potassium/phosphorus control, and the importance of never skipping dialysis sessions; verify with teach-back

opts.text: A palpable continuous thrill and audible continuous bruit over the maturing left-forearm fistula
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — a thrill and bruit confirm the access is patent and flowing; their presence is exactly what to verify.

opts.text: Interdialytic weight gain of 2.6 kg with pedal edema, bibasilar crackles, and pre-dialysis BP 168/92
opts.rat: RELEVANT — excess fluid/sodium intake between sessions causes volume overload and hypertension to address with dialysis and teaching.

opts.text: He is still dialyzing through a tunneled catheter while the fistula matures
opts.rat: RELEVANT — the catheter is the highest-infection access; transitioning to the fistula and removing it is a priority.

opts.text: A warm, pink hand with a strong distal pulse and brisk capillary refill on the access side
opts.rat: RELEVANT — normal distal perfusion indicates no vascular steal, a reassuring assessment finding to confirm and document.

opts.text: Pre-dialysis potassium 5.8 mmol/L after eating a banana and soup over the weekend
opts.rat: RELEVANT — hyperkalemia between sessions is dangerous and signals a need to reinforce potassium-restriction teaching.

opts.text: His blood pressure was measured in the right (non-access) arm
opts.rat: This is CORRECT practice, not a problem — BP must never be taken in the fistula arm, so this is reassuring, not a concerning cue.

opts.text: He took a multivitamin with breakfast this morning
opts.rat: Not clinically significant to the access or today's priorities (renal vitamins are commonly prescribed).

opts.text: A patent fistula shows a thrill and bruit; loss of either would suggest thrombosis and is an emergency
opts.rat: CORRECT — thrill/bruit indicate flow; their loss means the access is clotting and requires immediate escalation.

opts.text: The fistula is preferred over the catheter because it has lower infection and clotting risk; the catheter should be removed once the fistula is usable
opts.rat: CORRECT — 'fistula first': transitioning off the catheter reduces infection/sepsis and thrombosis risk.

opts.text: The access arm must be protected — no BP, venipuncture, IV, injections, or constriction
opts.rat: CORRECT — these protect flow and prevent damage/thrombosis of the fistula.

opts.text: The weight gain, edema, crackles, and hypertension reflect interdialytic fluid/sodium excess to manage with dialysis and diet teaching
opts.rat: CORRECT — volume overload between sessions is driven by fluid and sodium intake.

opts.text: Phosphate binders should be taken WITH meals, and potassium-rich foods/salt substitutes should be avoided
opts.rat: CORRECT — binders act on dietary phosphate in the gut; potassium restriction prevents dangerous hyperkalemia.

opts.text: Because dialysis removes wastes, he should follow a strict low-protein diet like a pre-dialysis CKD patient
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Dialysis removes protein, so dialysis patients need ADEQUATE protein — not the pre-dialysis protein restriction.

opts.text: It is fine to keep using the tunneled catheter long term since it is working
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The catheter carries the highest infection/clot risk; convert to the fistula and remove it as soon as feasible.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Rank these nursing priorities from #1 (HIGHEST) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Assess and protect the vascular access (confirm thrill/bruit, enforce access-arm precautions)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The access is the lifeline; verifying patency and preventing damage/thrombosis comes first.

opts.text: Address fluid overload and hyperkalemia through dialysis and reinforce fluid/sodium/potassium limits
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Volume overload and hyperkalemia are immediate physiologic risks corrected by dialysis and diet.

opts.text: Plan the transition from the catheter to the maturing fistula and minimize catheter infection risk
opts.rat: #3 — Reduces the largest ongoing infection/thrombosis risk once the fistula is reliably usable.

opts.text: Reinforce mineral-bone management (phosphate binders with meals, phosphorus restriction) and anemia therapy
opts.rat: #4 — Important chronic-management targets that prevent long-term complications.

opts.text: Provide ongoing education and teach-back on the overall self-care plan and never skipping sessions
opts.rat: #5 — Consolidates safe self-management once the immediate priorities are addressed.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED.

opts.text: Palpate for a thrill and auscultate for a bruit over the fistula and teach a daily self-check
opts.rat: Confirms patency and empowers the patient to detect thrombosis early.

opts.text: Place a limb-alert band and take blood pressures only in the non-access (right) arm
opts.rat: Protects the fistula from compression/damage that could cause thrombosis.

opts.text: Teach phosphate binders WITH meals, potassium restriction, and fluid/sodium limits with daily weights
opts.rat: Correct timing and dietary control prevent hyperphosphatemia, hyperkalemia, and volume overload.

opts.text: Use strict aseptic technique for the catheter and plan to remove it once the fistula is usable
opts.rat: Minimizes the catheter's high infection risk and supports the fistula-first transition.

opts.text: Hold the antihypertensive before dialysis and give dialyzable medications after the session per protocol
opts.rat: Prevents intradialytic hypotension and avoids dialyzing off the medication.

opts.text: Use the fistula arm for the next IV antibiotic and a blood draw since it has good access
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — never cannulate, draw from, or infuse into the fistula arm outside of dialysis; it risks the access.

opts.text: Apply a blood pressure cuff to the left (fistula) arm for trending
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — BP cuffs compress and can thrombose the fistula; use the opposite arm.

opts.text: Place the patient on a strict low-protein diet and have him take binders between meals
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — dialysis patients need adequate protein, and binders must be taken WITH meals to work.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the fistula (thrill/bruit, distal perfusion, signs of infection) and ensure the access arm is protected
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Confirming a functioning, protected access is the foundational priority.

opts.text: Measure pre-dialysis weight and vitals (BP in the non-access arm) and review the pre-dialysis potassium
opts.rat: #2 — Establishes fluid status and identifies hyperkalemia to be corrected by dialysis.

opts.text: Carry out the dialysis treatment to target dry weight, holding the antihypertensive beforehand per protocol
opts.rat: #3 — Removes excess fluid and potassium and corrects acidosis while avoiding intradialytic hypotension.

opts.text: Reinforce diet/fluid, phosphate-binder timing, and access-arm precautions with teach-back
opts.rat: #4 — Targets the behaviors that caused the overload/hyperkalemia and protect the access.

opts.text: Coordinate cannulating the maturing fistula and removing the catheter once it is reliably usable
opts.rat: #5 — Completes the fistula-first transition to eliminate the catheter's infection risk.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: Classify each finding as the situation IMPROVING, WORSENING, or UNCHANGED.

opts.text: After dialysis he is at dry weight with BP 138/82, edema reduced, and the fistula thrill/bruit intact
opts.rat: Fluid removed to target with a preserved, patent access is the desired outcome.

opts.text: At two weeks his interdialytic weight gains are smaller (~1.3 kg) and he takes binders with meals
opts.rat: Better fluid/sodium control and correct binder timing reflect effective self-management teaching.

opts.text: The fistula is successfully cannulated and the tunneled catheter is removed
opts.rat: Completing the fistula-first transition eliminates the catheter's high infection risk.

opts.text: After a BP cuff was repeatedly used on the fistula arm, the thrill and bruit are now absent and the area is firm
opts.rat: ⚠️ Loss of thrill/bruit indicates THROMBOSIS — a surgical emergency; notify immediately to attempt salvage.

opts.text: The catheter exit site is red with purulent drainage and he is febrile to 38.4°C
opts.rat: ⚠️ Signs of a catheter-related bloodstream infection — obtain cultures and escalate for antibiotics/line management.

opts.text: He reports the access hand is now cold, pale, painful, and numb
opts.rat: ⚠️ Vascular steal syndrome — the fistula is diverting arterial flow from the hand; report for possible intervention.

opts.text: Immediately after teaching, his understanding of which foods are high in potassium is still incomplete
opts.rat: Learning is in progress; continue reinforcement and re-evaluate understanding at the next visit.

opts.text: His hemoglobin remains 9.5 g/dL one week after the ESA dose
opts.rat: ESAs raise hemoglobin gradually over weeks; a stable value at one week is expected, not a treatment failure.

opts.text: At follow-up the mature fistula is reliably used each session and his potassium/phosphorus are controlled
opts.rat: A durable, well-functioning access with controlled labs confirms effective, safe long-term management.
